documents.t: This project provides a complete preprocessing pipeline for the FALCON Benchmark M1-A dataset, which includes primary motor cortex (M1) multi-unit activity (MUA) recordings and synchronously acquired 16-channel upper-limb intramuscular EMG (iEMG) signals from non-human primates performing a reach-to-grasp task.

This dataset is sourced from eLife 2023;12:e84296 and contains invasive neural recordings from the primary motor cortex (M1) of two rhesus macaques (Pop and Greyson) during power-grasp and key-grasp tasks. Neural signals were acquired via 96-channel Utah arrays, and behavioral data include grasping force signals (two-channel FSR) along with the corresponding forearm EMG signals.
| Category | Parameter | Default | GY classification demo | Jango regression demo | Description |
Data Source: Using adversarial networks to extend brain computer interface decoding accuracy over time (Dryad dataset). Includes data from multiple monkeys across different tasks (ISO/CO/RT/PG).
The Finger RL dataset contains single-neuron recordings from two tetraplegic human participants attempting individual finger movements.
Finger PPC is human posterior parietal cortex recordings during attempted finger movements.

This dataset contains sorted unit spiking times and behavioral data from a macaque performing a delayed reaching task. The experimental task was a center-out reaching task with obstructing barriers forming a maze, resulting in a variety of straight and curved reaches. Neural activity was recorded from electrode arrays implanted in the motor cortex (M1) and dorsal premotor cortex (PMd). Cursor position, hand position, and eye position were also recorded during the experiment, and hand velocity was calculated offline from hand position. The provided data has been limited to 100 train trials and 100 test trials. Provided as part of the Neural Latents Benchmark//neurallatents.github.io.

This project provides a preprocessing pipeline for the 'Data from: Nonlinear decoding models enable music reconstruction from human auditory cortex activity' dataset, transforming raw signals into formats suitable for model training. The dataset contains intracranial electroencephalogram (ECoG) data recorded from 29 patients with drug-resistant epilepsy while passively listening to Pink Floyd songs. The dataset contains preprocessed neural activity (high-frequency activity, 70-150 Hz), electrode coordinates (MNI template space), and auditory stimuli (raw waveform files, and auditory spectrograms at 32 and 128 frequency points). The HFA and the two auditory spectrograms were sampled at 100 Hz and temporally aligned (190.72 seconds duration).
Long-term 256-channel neural recordings from primary motor cortex (M1) of a macaque during an eight-direction center-out reaching task

This project provides a complete preprocessing pipeline for the FALCON Benchmark M2 dataset, which contains neural recordings from the primary motor cortex in non-human primates during finger movement tasks.

This project provides a preprocessing pipeline for the 'Data for: A high-performance speech neuroprosthesis' dataset, transforming raw signals into formats suitable for model training. The dataset consists of 12,100 sentences spoken by a single study participant. We use the "competitionData" version of it, which is a simplified version of the sentence data formatted and partitioned for machine learning research. It contains "train" and "test" partitions for model development, and a reserved "competitionHoldOut" set for the speech decoding competition (labels will be released after the competition). We currently only use the train (8800) versus test (880) partition data. For each sentence, a transcript of what the participant was trying to say was provided, along with a corresponding time series of neural spike activity recorded from 256 microelectrodes in speech related areas of the cortex.

Allen Brain Observatory — Visual Coding Neuropixels dataset (NWB, downloaded via AllenSDK). This dataset contains 58 experiments, each with up to 6 Neuropixels probes covering cortical, thalamic, and hippocampal regions, and is designed for systematic study of the mouse visual system under passive stimulation.
This project provides a complete preprocessing pipeline for Visual Grating Task ECoG recordings from neurotycho.org.

This project aims to conduct standardized preprocessing on the BOLD5000 dataset, extract fMRI features and perform dimensionality transformation, providing high-quality input features and corresponding labels for subsequent model training related to visual cognition (such as image classification, brain activity prediction, etc.).

This project provides a preprocessing pipeline for the EEG Motor Movement/Imagery dataset, transforming raw EEG signals into formats suitable for model training. Only two subsets of the EEG Motor Movement/Imagery dataset were used in the project. The first subset contains only performed left or right hand motor tasks. The second subset contains only imagined left or right hand motor tasks. In both subsets, a total of 103 out of 109 subjects were used, and subjects 38, 88, 89, 92, 100, and 104 were omitted due to data annotation errors.

Based on the requirements of the classification task, we only use the perceptionTraining subset (150 categories × 8 images = 1,200 natural images). This subset has undergone standard fMRI preprocessing in the original paper, including head motion correction, spatial registration, and global normalization, making it suitable for subsequent training-test splitting.
Repository Link: SEED-IV Dataset Official Website

This project provides a preprocessing method for the THINGS-EEG dataset, transforming raw EEG signals into model-ready data. The dataset contains high-temporal-resolution EEG responses from 10 participants to naturalistic object images, recorded using a Rapid Serial Visual Presentation (RSVP) paradigm.
This dataset is sourced from Zenodo Record 3854034, which contains invasive neural recordings from the primary motor cortex (M1) of non-human primates performing a center-out cursor control task.

BCIJelly Agent is an intelligent search agent that leverages individualized neural data to rapidly and automatically construct subject-specific decoding models. It automatically searches and combines optimal model configurations, drastically reducing the barrier to algorithm development.

The Agent system integrates multiple classical and cutting-edge decoding models, and innovatively introduces a Search Agent that can: Automatically search for optimal model architectures Combine different model components for best performance Build "personalized" models tailored to individual subjects Reduce manual tuning and experimentation effort

Automated Model Search: The agent explores the model space to find the best configuration for your data. Subject-Specific Optimization: Models are automatically adapted to individual neural patterns. Low-Code Interface: Minimal coding required to train and deploy high-performance decoding models.
See the Search Agent Appendix for detailed parameter tables and pseudocode. For broader context, explore the datasets and models sections.

The LLM reads three structured files (Task.md, Module.md and History.json), uses a pretrained shared encoder that is then frozen, and assembles and evaluates the candidate decoders. Decoders meeting the validation threshold proceed to hold-out testing and are recorded in a knowledge evolution step, whereas decoders below the threshold enter a diagnosis and design loop that proposes new modules for re-evaluation. Stopping conditions are shown on the right (at most 3 new module designs, 25 LLM calls, 60 minutes, or 2 consecutive rounds with no gain). Decoding performance across five invasive BCI datasets (Reaching B, Visual Coding, Jango, Indy and Finger PPC) spanning classification and regression tasks and recordings from humans, macaques and mice. Searched decoders under three LLM backends (DeepSeek-V4 Pro, ChatGPT-5.5 and Claude Opus-4.7) are compared with three state-of-the-art pretrained models (NDT1, NDT2 and POYO) and with our pretrained model (UniBCI34). Panels show the classification and regression tasks and the cross-species joint-training settings (human and macaque, human and mouse, macaque and mouse, and all three species together). Accuracy is reported for classification and the coefficient of determination (R2, as a percentage) for regression. The bars indicate the mean across recording days (or sessions, for datasets recorded in a single session), the dots show individual recording days, and the error bars indicate one standard deviation. Statistical significance was assessed using paired t tests. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001.

The shared encoder maps a neural input tensor of shape [N, T, C], where N is the number of trials, T is the number of time steps and C is the number of channels, to a shared feature representation of shape [N, T, H], where H is the hidden dimensionality. The input is first normalized per batch (per-batch z-norm) and passed through a per-task input projection, implemented either as a permutation-invariant Deepset module (a shared per-channel MLP followed by mean pooling over channels) or as a linear layer, producing a representation of shape [N, T, H]. A learnable global token r_task ∈ RH is prepended along the time dimension, yielding a sequence of shapes [N, T+1, H], to which a learnable positional encoding is added. This token allows all the time steps to exchange information through self-attention and is discarded after the final block. The sequence is processed by a stack of six Transformer blocks, each consisting of layer normalization, multi-head self-attention (8 heads) and a feed-forward network with residual connections, where H(l) denotes the hidden state after the lth block. After a final layer normalization, the global token is dropped to yield the per-time-step shared feature of shape [N, T, H], which is then passed to the task-specific decoders. Single-task decoding performance of closed-loop AAS with LLMs across five invasive BCI datasets. Each decoder is trained and evaluated separately on each of the five datasets (Jango, Indy, Finger PPC, Reaching B and Visual Coding) without joint training across datasets or species, in contrast to the cross-species joint-training settings in the cross-species results figure above. Models and the bar, dot and error bar conventions are as in that figure. Accuracy is used for the classification datasets (Finger PPC, Reaching B and Visual Coding), and the coefficient of determination (R2, shown as a percentage) is used for the regression datasets (Jango and Indy). Even in this single-task setting, the decoders searched by LLM-driven AAS remained competitive with the state-of-the-art pretrained models, exceeding them on Jango and performing comparably on Finger PPC, Reaching B and Visual Coding, whereas NDT1 retained an advantage on Indy. UniBCI was competitive on Finger PPC and Visual Coding, where it reached the highest mean performance. Statistical significance was assessed using paired t tests. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001.

The architecture search described thus far was designed for a single task at a time, and the module library was explored by random sampling. To handle multitask decoding and to replace random sampling with a guided design, we extended AAS with an LLM that plans, diagnoses and refines decoders in a closed loop (the workflow figure above). The agent reads three structured files that define the search context, a task specification (Task.md, covering the training, validation and test split, the species and the task type, including single-task and multitask decoding), a description of every available module (Module.md) and a running history of previously searched architectures with their parameter settings and validation and test scores (History.json). Guided by this context, the LLM first plans the search and pretrains a shared encoder on the training data, which is then frozen. It then enters a decoder search and selection stage, in which candidate decoders are assembled according to the plan and evaluated on the validation set. A decoder whose validation metric exceeds the threshold is subjected to hold-out testing, and its architecture and scores are written back to the history file in a knowledge evolution step. A decoder that falls below the threshold instead enters a diagnosis and design loop, in which the LLM examines why validation failed, designs a new module, checks that its shape is compatible with the data dimensions and returns it to the assembly stage for re-evaluation. The loop terminates under explicit stopping conditions, after which the accumulated knowledge is committed to the history file. We evaluated this LLM-driven search on five invasive BCI datasets (Reaching B, Visual Coding, Jango, Indy and Finger PPC) that together span classification and regression tasks and recordings from humans, macaques and mice. To isolate decoder search across the LLM backends, the same shared encoder architecture was used for all three models (the encoder architecture figure above), trained under the same data splits and frozen before the decoder search. Decoder search was then performed independently by DeepSeek-V4 Pro, ChatGPT-5.5 and Claude Opus-4.7, each of which planned and searched its own decoder. We compared these searched decoders against three state-of-the-art pretrained models (NDT1, NDT2 and POYO) and against our own pretrained model (UniBCI34), evaluating each on the single-task setting (the single-task results figure above), on the classification and regression tasks, and on the cross-species joint-training settings (human and macaque, human and mouse, macaque and mouse, and all three species together; the cross-species results figure above). Statistical significance was assessed using paired t tests. Across these settings, the encoder–decoder combinations produced by LLM-driven AAS were competitive with state-of-the-art pretrained models and exceeded them in selected cross-species settings (the cross-species results figure above). The greatest differences appeared in the cross-species joint-training settings, where on the Jango and Reaching B datasets the searched decoders scored significantly higher than the best pretrained baseline (paired t-test, P < 0.05). On the remaining datasets, and on the classification and regression tasks evaluated separately, the searched decoders and the best baselines did not differ significantly. On the Indy dataset, compared with the searched decoders, NDT1 scored significantly better under the macaque and mouse and the all-species settings. The three LLM backends did not differ significantly from one another. UniBCI performed competitively on the Finger PPC and Visual Coding datasets, matching the best pretrained baselines without a significant difference and achieving the highest numerical scores on Finger PPC in several settings. On the other datasets it scored below the state-of-the-art baselines.

LLM-driven AAS is exposed through bcijelly.LLMSearchAgent, which wraps the closed-loop search procedure described above. The API uses a task specification, module descriptions and a search history file to define the search context, and applies explicit stopping conditions for the maximum number of newly designed modules, LLM calls, wall-clock time and rounds without improvement. Given multitask data splits, LLMSearchAgent runs the guided decoder search, evaluates the candidate decoders on the specified validation sets, and writes the selected architectures and scores back to the history file. This provides a reproducible entry point for rerunning LLM-driven AAS with fixed data splits, module descriptions and search history. from bcijelly import LLMSearchAgent llm_agent = LLMSearchAgent( max_design_attempts = 3, max_llm_calls = 25, max_wall_clock_min = 60, no_improve_rounds = 2) multitask_data = llm_agent.split_data( data_path = "/data/invasive", split_mode = "csv") llm_agent.search( multitask_data, task_file = "./docs/task.md", module_files = "./docs/modules/", history_file = "./docs/history.json")

def load_data( dataset_name: str, *, protocol: str = "single_day", task: str | None = None, split_ratio: str = "7:1:2", subject_index: int = 0, day_index: int = 0, seed: int | None = 42, data_dir: Path | None = None, verbose: bool = False, auto_download_missing: bool = True, fallback_to_first_subject: bool = False, repo_id: str = DEFAULT_HF_REPO_ID, repo_type: str = DEFAULT_HF_REPO_TYPE, max_days_per_dataset: int | None = None, ) -> DataLoadResult

load_data is the unified data-loading API in BCIJelly. It automatically handles: Dataset discovery and availability checks (local or auto-download) Protocol/task compatibility validation Single-day or cross-day job construction NPZ reading with task-aware label key selection Train/val/test splitting and feature shape alignment Unified output as DataLoadResult

dataset_name​ Default: none (required). Role: selects which dataset directory to load. Impact: determines all downstream discovery, validation, job construction, and file loading branches. protocol​ Default: single_day. Supported: single_day, cross_day, train-test, train-val-test. Role: defines loading paradigm and split path. Key behavior: train-test and train-val-test run through single-day execution semantics. cross_day builds day-block train/val/test partitions. task​ Default: None, then fallback to classification. Supported: classification, regression, speech. Role: controls label-key selection and label-shape validation. Key behavior: classification expects (N,) labels. regression expects (N,T,2) labels with matching T to neural features. speech currently accepts (N,) or (N,L). split_ratio​ Default: 7:1:2. Role: controls train/val/test ratio. Rules: a:b means train:test. a:b:c means train:val:test. train and test ratios must both be non-zero. Notes: In cross-day, allocation is by day count, not random per-sample split. Under train-val-test, explicit val files may be required by dataset format/declaration. subject_index​ Default: 0. Role: selects subject subdirectory in multi-subject datasets. Notes: For non-multi-subject structures, only 0 is valid. day_index​ Default: 0. Role: selects which day/session job for single-day loading. Notes: For split-files format, only day_index=0 is valid. In cross-day, there is typically one job, so day_index usually stays 0. data_dir​ Default: None (internally falls back to data). Role: overrides dataset root location. auto_download_missing​ Default: True. Role: auto-downloads missing datasets from Hugging Face. fallback_to_first_subject​ Default: False. Role: when subject_index is out of range, fallback to first subject with warning instead of hard error. seed​ Default: 42. Role: controls reproducibility of random splits. Key note: Optional; when omitted, default value 42 is used. verbose​ Default: False. Role: prints dataset/job summaries for diagnostics. repo_id, repo_type​ Role: controls download source (defaults to official HF dataset repo). Typical use: private mirror/testing repository. max_days_per_dataset​ Role: caps discovered/constructed day count. Typical use: fast debugging and reduced-scale experiments.

import bcijelly as bj # single-day classification loaded = bj.load_data( dataset_name="1_FALCONM1-A", protocol="single_day", task="classification", subject_index=0, day_index=0, split_ratio="7:1:2", ) # cross-day classification loaded = bj.load_data( dataset_name="1_FALCONM1-A", protocol="cross_day", task="classification", subject_index=0, split_ratio="7:1:2", ) # single-day regression loaded = bj.load_data( dataset_name="5_Jango_force", protocol="single_day", task="regression", subject_index=0, day_index=0, split_ratio="7:1:2", ) # cross-day regression loaded = bj.load_data( dataset_name="5_Jango_force", protocol="cross_day", task="regression", subject_index=0, split_ratio="7:1:2", )

Main flow in src/bcijelly/dataset/_loading.py: Parameter and capability checks Validate protocol against capability declaration. Validate task (or fallback to classification). Parse split_ratio. Dataset structure discovery discover_dataset_structure identifies format: single_file split_files day_files multi_subject virtual_sessions (for VisualGratingTask) Dataset declaration compatibility validation If dataset_compatibility.json exists, validate requested protocol/task. Subject resolution Resolve subject branch via subject_index. Out-of-range behavior depends on fallback_to_first_subject. Branch A: split_files direct loading Fixed train/test (and optional val) file path flow. train-val-test may require explicit val file. Branch B: build jobs then choose one Build DatasetJob list via build_jobs. For single_day, select by day_index. For cross_day, there is usually one constructed job. Job-level loading (load_job_data) single_day: pre-split single-file keys -> direct train/test load. raw single-file -> split using split_ratio + seed. cross_day: load train/val/test day blocks. align to shared minimal (time, channels) shape. return aggregated test_dataset and per-day test_day_datasets. Return result Unified DataLoadResult fields: train_dataset val_dataset (optional) test_dataset test_day_datasets (common in cross-day) aligned_shape job requested_task If you want to dive deeper, start from: src/bcijelly/dataset/_loading.py src/bcijelly/dataset/_discovery.py src/bcijelly/dataset/_io.py

Left, the 80 modules currently included in BCIJelly, grouped into four functional categories (fully connected, backbones, convolutional and attention). Right, schematic showing how the same modules are combined to construct a range of established and recent decoding architectures collected in BCIJelly.
documents.s: The algorithmic module library in BCIJelly

documents.t: Given a neural recording, AAS first scans the module library, then runs the Agent Architecture Search (AAS) algorithm, which iteratively samples candidate architectures, trains each on a training set and evaluates it on a validation set. Candidates whose validation metric exceeds a predefined threshold are retained, the rest are discarded. The loop stops when K valid candidates have been collected or a maximum number of search rounds is reached, after which an ensemble selection step combines the retained candidates into a single model whose performance is reported on a held-out test set.

Each violin shows the distribution of test-day performance for one search, with markers indicating individual test days. Adjacent matrices report the number of overlapping modules between pairs of searches, showing that different runs converge to distinct module combinations. The best run reached a median accuracy of 0.71 on the classification task (11 test days spanning 35 days) and a median R² of 0.80 on the regression task (13 test days spanning 88 days).
documents.s: Performance of AAS across five independent searches

documents.t: This model corresponds to the best classification search result (search index = 5), which is an ensemble of four sub-networks. Each sub-network shares the same overall structure, starting from an InputProjection module and ending in a ClassificationHead, but differs in its intermediate feature-extraction modules, which combine components such as TripletAttention, CondConv, RepBlock, Involution, SKAttention and a ResNet18 backbone. The four sub-networks are combined into the final classification decoder.

This model corresponds to the best regression search result (search index = 5), consisting of a FlattenInput module followed by three fully connected layers (FlattenFC with 256, 128 and 128 units) and a RegressionHead.

The InputProjection module, which maps the input neural tensor of shape (C, T, N), where C is the number of channels, T the number of time steps and N the number of trials, into a hidden representation through a learned linear transformation with weight W and bias b.

The FlattenInput module, which reshapes the neural input for the fully connected regression network. The ClassificationHead, which applies average pooling over the temporal dimension followed by a linear projection (weight W of shape hidden_dim × num_class) to produce class scores.
The RegressionHead, which applies a linear projection (weight W of shape hidden_dim × C2, where C2 is the dimensionality of the continuous output) to produce the regression output.

# dynamic factories (examples) model = bcijelly.model.transformer(task="classification") model = bcijelly.model.lstm(task="regression") # capability query tasks = bcijelly.model.get_supported_tasks("transformer")

bcijelly.model provides dynamic model factory entry points and capability lookup utilities. Main responsibilities: Build model instances by model name + task. Validate model/task compatibility. Expose a unified adapter interface (BaseModel) with train and test methods.

Current factory names: KF MLP DFINE LSTM language LSTM transformer languageTransformer MSCFormer ndt1 ndt2 POYO lfads Cycle_Gan stabilization NoMAD MFSNN seqVAE FENet These names are resolved dynamically by module-level __getattr__.

get_supported_tasks​ def get_supported_tasks(model_name: str) -> tuple[str, ...] Role: return the declared supported tasks for a model. Example outputs: transformer -> ("classification", "regression", "speech") cycle_gan -> ("classification", "regression") create_model​ def create_model(*, model_name: str, task: str) -> BaseModel Role: construct a BaseModel instance after compatibility checks. Usually you call the dynamic factory instead of create_model directly.

transformer: classification, regression, speech lstm: classification, regression, speech cycle_gan: classification, regression lfads: classification, regression stabilization: classification, regression
import bcijelly model = bcijelly.model.transformer(task="classification") print(model.model_name) # transformer print(model.task) # classification
Data loading: load_data.md Train API: model.train.md Test API: model.test.md Summary API: summary.md
def test(self, *args: Any, **kwargs: Any) -> dict[str, Any]
model.test is the unified testing entrypoint on a model instance. Before delegating to backend test(...), the adapter restores train state captured by model.train(...) when available.

test_dataset​ Type: tuple, task-dependent. Role: in-memory test data. Required: yes, unless test_datapath is provided. verbose​ Type: bool. Role: print test-time details. test_datapath​ Type: str | None. Role: path-based test input.

LFADS classifier​ cleanup_temp_ckpt: bool = False Role: whether to remove temporary checkpoint artifacts after evaluation. LFADS regressor​ reduce: str = "center" Role: reduction mode aligned with LFADS regressor flow. CycleGAN classifier/regressor​ calib_ratio: float = 0.1 min_calib_trials: int = 5 max_calib_trials: int | None = 30 Role: split a calibration subset from test data before final evaluation. Stabilization classifier/regressor​ calib_ratio: float = 0.2 min_calib_trials: int = 1 max_calib_trials: int | None = None Role: calibration/evaluation split for stabilization adaptation path.
Classification/Regression backends usually expect: test_dataset: tuple[np.ndarray, np.ndarray] Speech backends expect: test_dataset: tuple[np.ndarray, np.ndarray, np.ndarray, np.ndarray, np.ndarray]
Backends rely on training state in-process; call model.train(...) before model.test(...) in the same runtime. Adapter-level state restore happens automatically before backend test call.
Type: dict[str, Any] Typical metric keys depend on task/backend: classification: often acc regression: often r2 some backends may also emit loss, mse, or mae

test_result = model.test( test_dataset=loaded_data.test_dataset, verbose=False, ) For cross-day workflows, this method runs one global test on merged test samples. You can also evaluate per day using: day_metric_list = [] for one_day_test_dataset in loaded_data.test_day_datasets: one_day_result = model.test(test_dataset=one_day_test_dataset, verbose=False) day_metric_list.append(float(one_day_result["acc"]))
Model factory: model.md Train API: model.train.md Summary API: summary.md
def train(self, *args: Any, **kwargs: Any) -> dict[str, Any]

model.train is the unified training entrypoint on a model instance. At adapter level it is *args/**kwargs, but the effective parameters are defined by backend model/task implementations. All backends support a concrete train API with named arguments, including train_dataset, val_dataset, seed, epochs, and more.

train_dataset​ Type: tuple, task-dependent. Role: in-memory training data. Required: yes, unless train_datapath is provided. val_dataset​ Type: tuple or None. Role: external validation input. batch_size​ Type: int. Typical defaults: 16, 32, or 64 depending on backend. epochs​ Type: int. Typical defaults: 6 to 400 depending on backend/task. lr​ Type: float. Typical defaults: 1e-4, 1e-3, 4e-3, etc. weight_decay​ Type: float. Role: optimizer regularization. seed​ Type: int. Default in all current backends: 42. device​ Type: str. Typical values: "auto", "cpu", "cuda". Role: runtime device selection. patience​ Type: int. Role: early-stop patience behavior where implemented. run_name​ Role: labels run outputs directory/name. output_dir​ Type: str. Default: "./outputs" (all current backends). Role: output root directory. log_csv​ Type: str | None. Default: None (all current backends). Role: optional custom CSV log path. save_ckpt​ Type: bool. Default: usually True. Exceptions: speech backends default to False: src/bcijelly/model/_lstm/speech.py src/bcijelly/model/_transformer/speech.py Role: whether to save checkpoints. plot​ Type: bool. Default: False (all current backends). Role: whether to generate training diagnostic plots. verbose​ Type: bool. Default: False (all current backends). Role: print extra runtime logs. train_datapath​ Type: str | None. Role: path-based training input (npz flow). Required: yes, if train_dataset is not provided. val_datapath​ Type: str | None. Role: path-based validation input. val_ratio​ Type: float. Role: internal validation split ratio for backends that support internal split.

Classification/Regression backends usually expect: train_dataset: tuple[np.ndarray, np.ndarray] val_dataset: tuple[np.ndarray, np.ndarray] Speech backends expect 5-tuples: train_dataset: tuple[np.ndarray, np.ndarray, np.ndarray, np.ndarray, np.ndarray] val_dataset: tuple[np.ndarray, np.ndarray, np.ndarray, np.ndarray, np.ndarray]

Transformer (classification/regression)​ num_layers: int d_model: int n_heads: int dropout: float Transformer (speech)​ Same core Transformer params plus: kernel_len: int stride_len: int gaussian_smooth_width: float white_noise_sd: float constant_offset_sd: float use_day_idx: bool LSTM (classification/regression)​ num_layers: int hidden_size: int dropout: float bidirectional: bool pooling: str (classifier backend) LSTM (speech)​ Same LSTM core params plus: kernel_len: int stride_len: int gaussian_smooth_width: float white_noise_sd: float constant_offset_sd: float use_day_idx: bool LFADS​ fac_dim: int dropout: float Regressor-only: reduce: str = "center" CycleGAN​ decoder_epochs: int decoder_lr: float g_lr: float d_lr: float source_ratio: float generator_hidden: int discriminator_hidden: int dropout_decoder: float dropout_g: float dropout_d: float identity_weight: float cycle_weight: float loss_type: str calib_ratio: float min_calib_trials: int max_calib_trials: int | None enable_alignment: bool Stabilization​ baseline_ntrials: int n_latents: int fa_n_restarts: int fa_ll_thresh: float fa_max_its: int fa_min_priv_var: float align_th: float align_n: int Classifier backend: pooling: str hidden_size: int enable_alignment: bool Regressor backend: hidden_sizes: Sequence[int] | None fine_tune: bool fine_tune_epochs: int

train_dataset and train_datapath are alternative input channels; at least one must be provided. External validation is enabled when val_dataset or val_datapath is provided. Some backends use val_ratio for internal split, while others require explicit external validation. After backend train(...) returns, adapter state can be captured for later model.test(...) reuse.
Type: dict[str, Any] Typical fields include training/validation metrics and run metadata (for example device/run directory). Exact keys are backend-dependent.

def summary( loaded_data: Any | None = None, model: Any | None = None, train_result: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None, test_result: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None, title: str = "Summary", mode: Literal["compact", "full"] = "compact", ) -> dict[str, Any]
summary builds, prints, and returns a user-facing summary from core pipeline results. It can produce: A compact overview for quick inspection. A full sectioned output for detailed diagnostics.

loaded_data​ Default: None. Role: output from bcijelly.load_data, used to extract dataset/job/shape metadata. model​ Default: None. Role: model instance used to infer module name, task type, and primary metric hints. train_result​ Default: None. Role: dictionary returned by model.train. test_result​ Default: None. Role: dictionary returned by model.test. title​ Default: Summary. Role: display title for compact print mode. mode​ Default: compact. Supported: compact, full. Role: controls printed detail level.

summary(...) returns a dictionary with fixed top-level sections: compact: key pipeline highlights. loaded_data: expanded load/job/shape details. module: model capability metadata. train_result: copied training result dictionary. test_result: copied testing result dictionary.

Summary metric display follows this order: Prefer model-declared PRIMARY_TEST_METRIC if present in test_result. Fallback scan of common keys: acc, r2, mse, mae, loss. For compact mode, metric row is shown in a merged text form similar to: acc: train ..., val ..., test ... or r2: train ..., val ..., test ...

compact_summary = bcijelly.summary( loaded_data=loaded_data, model=model, train_result=train_result, test_result=test_result, mode="compact", ) full_summary = bcijelly.summary( loaded_data=loaded_data, model=model, train_result=train_result, test_result=test_result, mode="full", )

PyTorch-trained ANN models are parsed into a hardware-ready computational graph that is then compiled for the target chip. SNN models pass through an additional node conversion step that translates PyTorch operators into spike-based equivalents, after which the converted graph follows the same parsing and compilation steps as ANNs. The choice of ANN or SNN deployment is set by a single argument to the conversion API.

Native operator coverage of three representative chips (left), a general-purpose GPU, the Lynxi neuromorphic chip and TaiBai, an in-house neuromorphic chip. Fully supported (green check), partially supported (half-orange) and not supported (red cross).

Real deployment of MFSNN on the three platforms (right): decoding accuracy is essentially identical across the GPU, Lynxi and TaiBai, confirming that operator conversion preserves model performance during deployment. Power consumption is reduced by roughly 30-fold on Lynxi (0.57 W) and 50-fold on TaiBai (0.34 W) compared with the GPU (16.48 W,NVIDIA RTX 4060).

Left, the original MFSNN architecture, in which long sequences of Conv2D, BN2D and LIF layers are interleaved with matrix multiplication, Hadamard product and matrix addition operations. Middle, after operator-level optimization, each Conv2D-BN2D-LIF sequence is fused into a single spike-conv2D operator that the target chip can execute natively. Right, after graph-level optimization, the computational graph is placed onto the core array of TaiBai with a reduction in core count from 113 to 33.
documents.s: Operator conversion and graph-level optimization of MFSNN during compilation

documents.t: The decoder takes a neural input tensor of shape [N, T, 96], where N is the number of trials, T the number of time steps and 96 the number of channels, and produces a continuous output of shape [N, T, 2], corresponding to the two-dimensional (x, y) position. The input is first mapped into a hidden representation by a fully connected projection and passed through a non-linear activation. The core of the network is a UFO Attention module identified by the architecture search (dashed box), in which the hidden representation x is projected into query, key and value through learned weights. The key and value branches are each followed by a cross-normalization step (XNorm), and the branches are combined through successive matrix multiplications to produce the attended representation. A further non-linear activation and a fully connected projection then map this representation to the two-dimensional output. This decoder generalizes across recording days, achieving successful cross-day decoding on the Jango dataset.
documents.s: Architecture of the hardware-deployment cross-day regression decoder identified by AAS

documents.t: Decoding performance and power consumption of three decoders deployed on a GPU and on the Lynxi neuromorphic chip, following the same deployment procedure.
documents.s: Deployment of three additional decoders on the Lynxi neuromorphic chip

documents.t: LSTM, a decoder collected in BCIJelly, evaluated on a classification task using the Finger PPC dataset. Decoding accuracy is 96.10% on both the GPU and Lynxi, while power consumption is reduced from 40.610 W to 1.033 W (roughly 39-fold).

FENet, also collected in BCIJelly, evaluated on a regression task using the Jango dataset. The decoding performance (R²) is 0.8845 on the GPU and 0.8836 on Lynxi, while power consumption is reduced from 31.415 W to 0.606 W (roughly 52-fold).

A decoder searched by AAS, was evaluated on the same regression task. R² is 0.8499 on both platforms, while power consumption is reduced from 31.714 W to 0.872 W (roughly 36-fold). Across all three decoders, decoding performance on the GPU and Lynxi is essentially identical, confirming that operator conversion preserves model performance during deployment. These results demonstrate that the BCIJelly toChip pipeline generalizes across both collected and automatically generated decoders.

documents.t: Recording Modalities: Simultaneous acquisition of Electrocorticography (ECoG) and eye-tracking data during task performance. No motion tracking was conducted.

Task Type: Passive Viewing Task. Stimuli: Six distinct movie clips. Subject State: The subject (macaque) remained seated in a head-fixed configuration to minimize motion artifacts and ensure the stability of visual stimulation. Feedback & Reward Mechanism: No food reward was provided. The subject was not required to perform any active behavioral interactions, thereby eliminating potential confounding effects from operant conditioning. Synchronized Data Acquisition: Neural Recording: Cortical ECoG signals were recorded at a sampling rate of 1000 Hz. Behavioral Recording: Eye-tracking data were acquired at a sampling rate of 120 Hz. Synchronization & Constraints: Neural and behavioral signals were strictly synchronized via hardware/software triggers. The experimental design explicitly excluded limb motion tracking, focusing the analytical scope on the mapping between visual input, oculomotor behavior, and cortical neural dynamics.
This workflow processes 128-channel ECoG LFP data and converts it into a binned spike representation suitable for classification and pattern recognition tasks.

This project provides a complete preprocessing pipeline for the FALCON Benchmark M1-A dataset, which includes primary motor cortex (M1) multi-unit activity (MUA) recordings and synchronously acquired 16-channel upper-limb intramuscular EMG (iEMG) signals from non-human primates performing a reach-to-grasp task. The goal is to convert raw data into synchronized, sliding-window neural activity samples with behavioral labels (e.g., object category, target position), making them suitable for training machine learning models.
Species: Macaca mulatta Number of subjects: 3

Neural Input: Multi-unit activity (MUA) captured from the primary motor cortex (M1) using chronically implanted electrode arrays. Muscle Output: 16-channel intramuscular EMG (iEMG) signals, synchronized with neural data to capture the activation patterns of upper-limb muscles during task execution.

Type: Reach-to-grasp, center-out reaching task. Conditions: 4 different objects 8 possible target positions Behavioral events recorded: gocue_time move_onset_time contact_time reward_time result, number, tgt_loc, tgt_obj, obj_id, condition_id

This dataset is sourced from eLife 2023;12:e84296 and contains invasive neural recordings from the primary motor cortex (M1) of two rhesus macaques (Pop and Greyson) during power-grasp and key-grasp tasks. Neural signals were acquired via 96-channel Utah arrays, and behavioral data include grasping force signals (two-channel FSR) along with the corresponding forearm EMG signals.
Species: Rhesus macaque Number of subjects: 2 (monkeys P and G)

Implantation: Both monkeys were chronically implanted with 96-channel Utah electrode arrays in the hand or arm representation area of the primary motor cortex (M1). Hardware channels: 96-channel electrode arrays for neural activity recording. Processing: Recordings contain multi-unit activity (MUA) based on threshold crossings. Spike counts were binned in 50 ms non-overlapping windows, then smoothed with a Gaussian kernel (S.D. = 100 ms) to obtain firing rate estimates for each channel. EMG data: Forearm and hand muscle electromyographic (EMG) signals were recorded synchronously, differentially amplified, band-pass filtered (4-pole, 50–500 Hz), rectified, low-pass filtered (4-pole, 10 Hz, Butterworth), subsampled to 20 Hz, and then resampled to 1000 Hz to align with neural data. Force data: Grasping forces were measured by a pair of force-sensitive resistors (FSRs) attached to the sides of the grasping gadgets. Cursor position was determined by the sum (vertical axis) and difference (horizontal axis) of the two FSR outputs.

Task: Grasping task, in which monkeys reached to grasp a gadget placed under the screen with one hand. monkey P: the gadget was a cylinder, facilitating a power grasp with the palm and fingers. monkey G: the gadget was a small rectangular cuboid, facilitating a key grasp with the thumb and index finger. Trial structure: At the beginning of each trial, the monkey kept the hand resting on a touch pad for a random time (0.5–1.0 s). Successful holding triggered the onset of one of three possible rectangular targets on the screen and an auditory go cue. The monkey was required to place the cursor into the target and hold for 0.6 s by increasing and maintaining the grasping force applied on the gadget to obtain a liquid reward. Trial extraction: Trials were extracted from gocue time to trial end (reward time), since the monkeys' movements were quite random before the go cue.

Neural data original sampling rate: 1000 Hz (bin_width = 1 ms) For Pop, the distributed data have been pre-binned into 50 ms bins (after smoothing) EMG resampled to 1000 Hz and synchronized with neural data

Search budget max_models_per_round 8 4 6 Maximum number of candidate models sampled and trained in each search round. Search budget max_search_rounds 4 3 3 Maximum number of search rounds. The search may stop earlier if enough candidates are retained. Candidate filtering val_threshold 0.75 0.78 0.78 Validation metric threshold. Candidates below this threshold are discarded from the retained pool. Candidate filtering retain_top_k 6 4 4 Maximum number of validation-qualified candidates retained for ensemble selection. Ensemble selection ensemble_top_m 4 4 4 Number of top retained candidates considered for ensemble subset selection. Ensemble selection ensemble_method auto auto mean Ensemble rule. Classification supports mean, stacking, and auto; regression uses mean output averaging. Sampling constraint max_per_type 2 2 2 Maximum number of modules from the same module type in a candidate architecture. Sampling constraint allow_repeat True True True Whether repeated module keys are allowed inside one candidate architecture. Sampling constraint min_distinct_types 1 2 1 Minimum number of distinct module types required in one candidate architecture. Sampling constraint no_repeat_within_types None backbone backbone Module types that are not allowed to repeat within one candidate architecture. Training batch_size 16 16 16 Mini-batch size for candidate model training and evaluation. Training epochs 12 12 18 Maximum training epochs for each candidate model. Training patience 4 4 5 Early stopping patience for candidate model training. Training learning_rate 1e-3 1e-3 8e-4 Adam optimizer learning rate. Training weight_decay 1e-4 1e-4 1e-4 Adam optimizer weight decay. Training normalize_regression_target True True True Whether regression targets are normalized using training-set statistics only.

Algorithm: Validation-driven architecture search and ensemble selection Input: Training data D_train = (X_train, y_train) Validation data D_val = (X_val, y_val) Task type task_type in {classification, regression} Search configuration: max_models_per_round, max_search_rounds, max_depth, val_threshold, retain_top_k, ensemble_top_m Output: Best combined model f_best Search summary containing retained candidates and the selected ensemble 1. Discover candidate modules from the local model library. 2. Initialize the retained candidate set P = empty. 3. For round r = 1 to max_search_rounds: 4. Sample up to max_models_per_round candidate architectures under the search constraints. 5. For each candidate architecture a: 6. Build a time-series model: InputProjection -> searched module blocks -> task head. 7. Train the model on D_train. 8. Evaluate the model on D_val. 9. If the validation metric of a is at least val_threshold: 10. add a to P. 11. Keep only the top retain_top_k candidates in P according to validation metric. 12. If |P| >= retain_top_k: 13. stop the search. 14. If P is empty: 15. use the best successfully trained candidates as the fallback pool. 16. Select the top ensemble_top_m candidates from P. 17. Evaluate candidate ensembles on D_val: classification: combine class probabilities by mean or stacking; regression: average predicted trajectories. 18. Choose the ensemble with the best validation metric. 19. Return the selected ensemble as f_best. External evaluation: 20. Apply f_best to each held-out test day. 21. Report day-wise metric, average metric, median metric, and minimum metric.

This dataset contains sorted unit spiking times and behavioral data from a macaque performing a delayed reaching task. The experimental task was a center-out reaching task with obstructing barriers forming a maze, resulting in a variety of straight and curved reaches. Neural activity was recorded from electrode arrays implanted in the motor cortex (M1) and dorsal premotor cortex (PMd). Cursor position, hand position, and eye position were also recorded during the experiment, and hand velocity was calculated offline from hand position. The provided data has been limited to 100 train trials and 100 test trials. Provided as part of the Neural Latents Benchmark: https://neurallatents.github.io.
Species: Rhesus macaque Number of subjects: 1 (monkey J)

Implantation: One 96-channel Utah electrode array was chronically implanted in the primary motor cortex (M1) and another in the dorsal premotor cortex (PMd), totaling 192 channels. Hardware channels: 2 × 96-channel Utah arrays (M1: 96 ch, PMd: 96 ch). Processing: Raw signals were sampled at 30000 Hz and offline spike-sorted to obtain timestamps of single-unit or multi-unit activity. Data are stored as spike times (in seconds) without binning or smoothing, preserving maximum flexibility. Behavioral data: Two-dimensional cursor position (cursor_pos), hand position (hand_pos), hand velocity (hand_vel), and eye position (eye_pos) were recorded synchronously.

Task: Delayed center-out reaching with maze barriers. The monkey moved a cursor from the center to a peripheral target, but barriers could be placed along the path, eliciting either straight or curved reach trajectories. Trial structure: Fixation on center point → target presentation (with possible barriers/distractors) → variable delay period → go cue → reach to target and hold. Maze configurations: 27 maze configurations in total (maze_id 0–26); this subset (MC_Maze-Small) uses 9 of them. Each trial may contain 0–9 barriers and 1–3 targets. Trial extraction: Trials are segmented by their time intervals (start_time to stop_time). Training trials are full-length (~3 s/trial); test trials are cropped equal-length snippets (0.7 s/trial, extracted from trials).

This project provides a preprocessing pipeline for the 'Data from: Nonlinear decoding models enable music reconstruction from human auditory cortex activity' dataset, transforming raw signals into formats suitable for model training. The dataset contains intracranial electroencephalogram (ECoG) data recorded from 29 patients with drug-resistant epilepsy while passively listening to Pink Floyd songs. The dataset contains preprocessed neural activity (high-frequency activity, 70-150 Hz), electrode coordinates (MNI template space), and auditory stimuli (raw waveform files, and auditory spectrograms at 32 and 128 frequency points). The HFA and the two auditory spectrograms were sampled at 100 Hz and temporally aligned (190.72 seconds duration).
Subject: Patients with drug-resistant (pharmacoresistant) epilepsy. Number of subjects: 29
Experimental Task: Passive listening to the song by Pink Floyd.

Long-term 256-channel neural recordings from primary motor cortex (M1) of a macaque during an eight-direction center-out reaching task
Species: Crab-eating macaque Number of subjects: 1

Implantation: A 256-channel electrode array (232 effective channels) was chronically implanted in the primary motor cortex (M1). Hardware channels: 256-channel electrode array (232 effective channels). Processing: Raw signals were sampled at 30000 Hz. Long-term recording: Data were collected across two distinct recording periods — the first lasting 105 days and the second lasting 163 days, separated by a 31-day interval during which the electrode array was replaced.

Task: Eight-direction center-out reaching task. Each trial consists of four sequential phases: Initial phase: a central point appears on the screen, prompting the monkey to move its hand to the center in preparation for the task. Reaching phase: the center point disappears after successful contact, and a target point is displayed, requiring the monkey to move its hand from the center to the target; Confirmation phase: the monkey must maintain contact with the target for ~50 ms to confirm a successful touch; Reward phase: a juice reward is provided upon successful completion. Trial classification: Trials are classified into eight types based on target direction, numbered sequentially in counterclockwise order. Trial duration: ~2 s per trial. Trial independence: Trials are conducted independently.

Species: Rhesus macaque Number of subjects: 2 (monkeys C and M)

Implantation: Both monkeys were chronically implanted with 96-channel Utah electrode arrays in the primary motor cortex (M1) or dorsal premotor cortex (PMd). Hardware channels: 96-channel electrode arrays for neural activity recording. Processing: Recordings contain multi-unit activity (MUA) based on threshold crossings. Spike counts were binned in 50 ms non-overlapping windows, then smoothed with a Gaussian kernel (S.D. = 100 ms) to obtain firing rate estimates for each channel. Kinematic data: 2D cursor position, velocity, and acceleration of the manipulandum endpoint were recorded synchronously in real time.

Task: Center-Out (CO) reaching task, in which monkeys grasped the upright handle of a planar manipulandum and operated it with the upper arm in a parasagittal plane. Chewie (monkey C): performed the task with the right hand. Mihili (monkey M): performed the task with the left hand. Trial structure: At the beginning of each trial, the monkey moved the hand to the center of the workspace. After a random waiting period, one of eight outer targets equally spaced on a circle was presented. The monkey held during a variable delay period until receiving an auditory go cue. The monkey was required to reach the outer target within 1.0 s and hold within the target for 0.5 s to receive a liquid reward. Trial extraction: Trials were extracted from gocue to trial end (reward time), since the monkeys remained static before the go cue.

This project provides a preprocessing pipeline for the 'Data for: A high-performance speech neuroprosthesis' dataset, transforming raw signals into formats suitable for model training. The dataset consists of 12,100 sentences spoken by a single study participant. We use the "competitionData" version of it, which is a simplified version of the sentence data formatted and partitioned for machine learning research. It contains "train" and "test" partitions for model development, and a reserved "competitionHoldOut" set for the speech decoding competition (labels will be released after the competition). We currently only use the train (8800) versus test (880) partition data. For each sentence, a transcript of what the participant was trying to say was provided, along with a corresponding time series of neural spike activity recorded from 256 microelectrodes in speech related areas of the cortex.

Allen Brain Observatory — Visual Coding Neuropixels dataset (NWB, downloaded via AllenSDK). This dataset contains 58 experiments, each with up to 6 Neuropixels probes covering cortical, thalamic, and hippocampal regions, and is designed for systematic study of the mouse visual system under passive stimulation.
Recording Sites: Spanning multiple brain regions including the cortex, thalamus, and hippocampus

Standardized Stimulation Protocol: A standardized visual stimulation protocol was administered to each subject (mouse) to ensure temporal reproducibility across experiments and to facilitate the parallel mapping of visual responses across diverse brain regions. Characterization of Classical Stimulus Features: Stimulus Content: Comprised of Gabor patches, full-field drifting gratings, and moving dots. Experimental Objective: Aimed at precisely characterizing the tuning properties of individual neurons, such as orientation selectivity or spatial frequency preference. Ecologically Relevant Experiments: Stimulus Content: A series of complex natural images and natural movies were presented. Experimental Objective: To explore the synergistic interactions and population dynamics across different visual cortical areas within an ecologically relevant and realistic context.

This project provides a complete preprocessing pipeline for Visual Grating Task ECoG recordings from neurotycho.org.
The experiment synchronously recorded the subject’s cortical electroencephalography (ECoG) data and eye position information using a 128-channel implanted microelectrode array.

Experimental Task: Subjects were head-fixed with both arms restrained. ECoG signals and eye positions were recorded simultaneously. Visual Stimulation: A monitor was placed in front of the monkey, displaying a grating pattern moving in eight different directions. Stimulus events are encoded as analog values and can be decoded from the 129th channel. Trial Structure: The monkey was not required to fixate on the screen. Blank screens and stimulus patterns alternated every 2 seconds. Physical Parameters: The sinusoidal grating had a spatial period of 27 mm and moved at a velocity of 108 mm/s (4 Hz). The distance between the monkey and the screen was 490 mm.

This project aims to conduct standardized preprocessing on the BOLD5000 dataset, extract fMRI features and perform dimensionality transformation, providing high-quality input features and corresponding labels for subsequent model training related to visual cognition (such as image classification, brain activity prediction, etc.).
Functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging (fMRI) records the temporal distribution of BOLD signal intensity within multiple visually relevant regions of interest (ROIs: PPA, RSC, OPA, LOC, EarlyVis).

Visual Stimulus Task: In a single trial, a visual stimulus image is presented to the participant, with each image corresponding to a regressor (1s ON, 9s OFF). The experimental images are sourced from three categories/datasets: ImageNet, COCO, and Scenes (SUN). Each fMRI run consists of 37 visual stimulus images.

Technique: Mesoscopic single-photon / wide-field calcium imaging, used to record fluorescent signals from the cortex of head-fixed mice. The spatial resolution of the images is adjusted to 128 × 128 pixels.

Neural Activity and Behavior Synchronization: Neural activity and behavioral performance are synchronously recorded to analyze the relationship between cortical dynamics and behavior. Behavior Definition: Binary labeling based on the mouse’s movement speed: frames with movement speed > 0.5 cm/s are defined as “running” state, while frames with movement speed < 0.5 cm/s are defined as “resting” state. Experiment Scale: A total of 64 experimental sessions, with each session lasting 10 minutes. The number of sessions for the 5 mice are 11, 12, 14, 15, and 12 respectively.
The calcium imaging frame rate is 30 frames per second (30 fps). Each 10-minute experimental session generates approximately 18,000 frames of image data.

This project provides a preprocessing pipeline for the EEG Motor Movement/Imagery dataset, transforming raw EEG signals into formats suitable for model training. Only two subsets of the EEG Motor Movement/Imagery dataset were used in the project. The first subset contains only performed left or right hand motor tasks. The second subset contains only imagined left or right hand motor tasks. In both subsets, a total of 103 out of 109 subjects were used, and subjects 38, 88, 89, 92, 100, and 104 were omitted due to data annotation errors.

The BCI2000 system was used to record 64-channel EEG signals. The physical electrode layout strictly follows the international 10-10 system (excluding peripheral or specific electrodes such as Nz, F9, F10, FT9, FT10, A1, A2, TP9, TP10, P9, and P10).

The experiment primarily focuses on Motor Execution and Motor Imagery. Participants are required to respond according to target cues displayed on the screen. Each participant must complete 14 experimental runs, which include: Baseline state: 2 runs of 1 minute each (eyes closed resting, eyes open resting). Task state: 12 runs of 2 minutes each (the following 4 tasks are repeated 3 times): Task 1: Execute opening and closing of the left or right fist based on left-right cues displayed on the screen. Task 2: Imagine opening and closing the left or right fist based on left-right cues displayed on the screen. Task 3: Execute opening and closing of both fists (up) or both feet (down) based on up-down cues displayed on the screen. Task 4: Imagine opening and closing both fists (up) or both feet (down) based on up-down cues displayed on the screen.
160 Hz (i.e., 160 data samples are collected per second per channel).

Based on the requirements of the classification task, we only use the perceptionTraining subset (150 categories × 8 images = 1,200 natural images). This subset has undergone standard fMRI preprocessing in the original paper, including head motion correction, spatial registration, and global normalization, making it suitable for subsequent training-test splitting.

fMRI (Functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging): Each scanning session includes: Functional images (EPI): Covering the entire brain. Anatomical images (in-plane T2 weighted): Acquired using the same slices as the EPI. High-resolution anatomical reference images (T1 weighted): Scanned only once for each participant, used as structural references.

The experiment is divided into two main types of tasks: Image Presentation (Perception) and Imagery (Representation) tasks. Image Presentation Task (Perception Task): Participants view object images from ImageNet and perform a 1-back image repetition task. Training Set (perceptionTraining): Contains 150 object categories, with 8 images per category, totaling 1,200 natural images. Each image is presented only once (24 runs in total, with 3-5 sessions). Test Set (perceptionTest): Contains 50 object categories, with 1 image per category, totaling 50 images. Each image is repeated 35 times (35 runs in total, with 4-6 sessions). Imagery Task (imageryTest): Participants are asked to mentally visualize one of the 50 categories from the test set. There are 20 runs in total (3-5 scanning sessions), with each run containing 25 categories, and 10 samples per category. Participants are also required to self-rate the vividness of their mental imagery.

EEG (Electroencephalography): EEG signals were recorded using a 62-channel ESI NeuroScan system. Eye-tracking: Eye movement data were synchronously collected using SMI eye-tracking glasses.

Each session consists of 24 trials. In each trial, participants view a movie clip that has been pre-screened. These 72 movie clips are designed to induce one of four target emotions: happiness, sadness, fear, or neutral.
The raw EEG signals were downsampled to 200 Hz before feature extraction. Band-pass Filtering: A 1 Hz to 75 Hz band-pass filter was applied to remove noise and artifacts.

This project provides a preprocessing method for the THINGS-EEG dataset, transforming raw EEG signals into model-ready data. The dataset contains high-temporal-resolution EEG responses from 10 participants to naturalistic object images, recorded using a Rapid Serial Visual Presentation (RSVP) paradigm. Each participant completed 82,160 trials, covering 16,740 image conditions. The training set consists of 16,540 (image categories) × 10 (images per category) × 4 (repetitions), and the test set contains 200 (image categories) × 1 (image) × 80 (repetitions). Training and test stimuli were presented in a pseudo-random order, and target images were used to reduce blinking and other artifacts. EEG was recorded with a 64-channel EASYCAP (electrodes arranged according to the standard 10–10 system) and a BrainVision actiCHamp amplifier at 1000 Hz sampling rate, with online filtering (0.1–100 Hz) and referencing to the Fz electrode.

Acquisition Hardware: A 64-channel EASYCAP EEG cap (electrode layout following the international 10-10 system) paired with a Brainvision actiCHamp amplifier. Online Processing: During data acquisition, a real-time 0.1 Hz to 100 Hz band-pass filter is applied online. Reference Electrode: The Fz electrode is used as the online reference.

Core Paradigm: Rapid Serial Visual Presentation (RSVP) paradigm. Stimulus Content: Object images presented against natural backgrounds. Quality Control Mechanism: “Target images” are interspersed within the image sequence to control participant attention and reduce the impact of blinking and other motion artifacts on the EEG signals. Experiment Scale: Each participant performs 82,160 trials (the training set includes 16,540 image categories, and the test set includes 200 image categories).

Cycle-GAN (Cycle Generative Adversarial Network) is innovatively introduced here as an unsupervised neural Domain Adaptation aligner. In the long-term application of Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI), neural spike data recorded by microelectrode arrays often experience non-stationary drift in the feature space due to minor electrode displacements or natural neuronal evolution. Cycle-GAN breaks through the traditional limitation of having to rely on a large amount of labels from the target domain to retrain downstream decoders. Its core mechanism lies in: by constructing a bidirectional mapping, it seamlessly aligns the drifted neural spike features of the target day back to the feature space of the source day, thereby achieving calibration-free reuse of the pre-trained decoder. From the perspective of theory and code architecture, this Cycle-GAN alignment system consists of the following four key modules: Bidirectional Feature Generators The model instantiates two symmetrical generators, both adopting a three-layer Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) architecture, and integrating dropout and activation mechanisms. The generators are responsible for learning the non-linear mapping between high-dimensional neural feature manifolds, converting source domain features to target domain features, or restoring target domain features back to the source domain. Adversarial Discriminators The system contains two independent discriminator modules (also three-layer structures, outputting a single node), which are respectively responsible for distinguishing real neural features from the aligned features forged by the generators. By calculating the adversarial loss, the discriminators and generators drive the aligned features to approximate the true neural firing distribution in a minimax game. Cycle & Identity Constraints To prevent the generators from losing core kinematic or intentional information when mapping high-dimensional neural spikes, the model mandatorily introduces two key penalty mechanisms: Cycle Loss: Ensures that features can precisely reconstruct the original input after undergoing a dual mapping (from the source domain to the target domain, and back to the source domain). Identity Loss: When the generator receives intra-domain features as input, it constrains the output to remain unchanged, avoiding excessive distortion of the basal neural signals. Decoupled Downstream Decoders The system removes the coupling constraints between the aligner and the decoder. It provides a regressor (using a smooth loss) for fitting continuous kinematic signals (e.g., force, electromyography), and a classifier (using cross-entropy loss, with a built-in gradient clipping mechanism to prevent explosion) for discrete action/state recognition.

This system has established a rigorous staged validation and metric tracking pipeline. Model debugging centers on the following generalization assessments: Multidimensional evaluation for regression tasks: The core metric is the multi-output variance-weighted coefficient of determination. For two-dimensional kinematic decoding, the approximation quality along each independent coordinate axis is additionally decoupled and reported separately. Accuracy monitoring for classification tasks: Focus is placed on classification accuracy, as its improvement directly reflects the effective overlap of the feature manifolds. Periodic validation and checkpoint persistence: After a specified number of iterations, the model enters validation mode to track the performance on a validation set (using a frozen source-domain decoder fed with aligned inputs). The weights of the best-performing generator and decoder are automatically saved. End-to-end logging and post-processing: Daily validation results are aggregated into tabular files. The system supports lossless mapping of headerless data matrices back to tensor files via scripts, facilitating subsequent analysis.

Leveraging its unpaired domain adaptation capability, this Cycle-GAN architecture precisely addresses the most critical pain points in the current field of long-term implantable BCIs: Unsupervised Zero-Retraining Alignment Calibration The system abandons the conventional approach of modifying large downstream decoders and instead employs an unsupervised Cycle-GAN framework to pull drifted inputs back into the original feature space. As a result, it requires no labeled calibration data; with only a small amount of unlabeled neural sequences, it can perform adaptive alignment in the background, completely eliminating the costly burden of daily model retraining. Cross-Task Generalization and Decoupled Offline/Online Deployment The unified feature aligner exhibits strong versatility, seamlessly driving highly heterogeneous downstream tasks (e.g., regression or classification). The architecture is perfectly decoupled by design: the extremely time-consuming generative adversarial training is confined to the offline phase. Once the weights are frozen, the real-time mapped data stream can be fed into the downstream network with zero latency, ensuring highly robust online real-time closed-loop operation.

DFINE is a standalone regression and state inference framework specifically designed for processing complex time-series dynamical systems (e.g., neural population signals). Its core concept lies in combining the nonlinear feature extraction capability of deep learning with the rigorous mathematical derivations of classical linear dynamical systems (such as the Kalman Filter/Smoother), enabling dimensionality reduction of high-dimensional data while explicitly capturing the temporal evolution patterns of latent states, thereby achieving highly robust continuous trajectory tracking. From the code implementation and network topology, the present DFINE architecture consists of the following core modules: Multilayer Perceptron Feature Mapping Network (MLP Encoder, Decoder & Mapper) The model’s underlying layers do not rely on traditional convolutional or recurrent networks; instead, they adopt multiple sets of highly flexible multilayer perceptron (MLP) components. Among them, the encoder is responsible for nonlinearly projecting the high-dimensional raw observation sequence into a low-dimensional latent space (a_hat); the decoder is responsible for reconstructing the original observations from the latent states to ensure that core information is not lost; and the dedicated mapper network maps the smoothed internal hidden states into the final external behavioral or kinematic variables (behv_hat). Core Linear Dynamical System Module (Linear Dynamical Model, LDM) As the “heart” for capturing temporal dependencies, the architecture deeply integrates a differentiable linear dynamical model (LDM). This module is built with a rigorous state transition matrix (A), emission matrix (C), and learnable diagonal covariance parameters. It performs operations similar to Kalman filtering during forward propagation, and executes backward smoothing when do_smoothing=True is enabled. This mechanism allows the network to infer extremely smooth latent state trajectories that conform to physical/physiological dynamical evolution laws from noisy observational data. Multi-step Prediction and Multi-objective Joint Decoding Mechanism (Multi-step Prediction & Joint Optimization) In terms of optimization objectives, the model bypasses a single regression loss and adopts an ambitious composite optimization strategy. The loss function includes not only the observation reconstruction error (recon_mse) and the behavioral decoding error (behv_mse), but also innovatively introduces a K-step ahead prediction error (pred_mse, controlled by steps_ahead) based on the state transition matrix. This forces the model to learn an internal dynamic representation within the latent space that possesses strong forward-looking inference capability.

The debugging and evaluation pipeline of the DFINE model is centrally integrated within regressor.py: Core Evaluation Metrics (Multidimensional Regression Diagnostics) Simultaneously computes the mean squared error (MSE), mean absolute error (MAE), and the coefficient of determination (R2) in the original physical units. Among these, the validation R2 (best_val_r2) serves as the absolute core benchmark for triggering early stopping and optimal-weight checkpoint snapshots. Core Debugging Techniques Forced gradient clipping: clip_grad_norm_ is enforced prior to the optimizer step to circumvent gradient explosion, substantially improving training stability on low signal-to-noise ratio data. Stepped learning rate with dynamic early stopping: Coupled with the Adam optimizer, a StepLR scheduler is employed to periodically decay the learning rate and prevent oscillations; a tolerance-based early stopping mechanism is also built in. Runtime adaptive calibration: Online fine-tuning is supported during the testing phase. The calib_scope parameter provides fine-grained control over the unfrozen range (e.g., shallow mapper layers), and a minimal amount of data (calib_ratio) is used for rapid alignment, effectively preventing catastrophic forgetting.

In conjunction with the code logic of DFINE and its input tensor assertions (which strictly require the regression target to have the shape (N, T, 2)), this architecture primarily targets the following cutting-edge application scenarios: High-Fidelity Continuous Decoding and Latent Dynamics Discovery By leveraging an LDM to extract smooth latent states and explicitly fitting a state transition matrix, the model not only effectively filters out noise and produces exceptionally smooth two-dimensional continuous motion control trajectories, but also acts as a computational microscope, uncovering and visualizing the underlying low-dimensional neural dynamical manifold. Cross-Day Non-Stationary Online Ultra-Fast Adaptation To address the most challenging issue of cross-day signal baseline drift, built-in normalization strategies are combined with test-time ultra-fast online calibration. Prior to clinical deployment, only minimal data are required to fine-tune the shallow network layers, perfectly reconciling the long-term memory of core dynamics with the immediate flexibility of the mapping, thereby significantly extending the system's operational lifespan.

Participants: 20 participants (10 male, 10 female), aged 19-26 years (mean age = 22.5 years, standard deviation = 1.8). Conditions: All participants are right-handed, with normal or corrected-to-normal vision and normal hearing. Screening: Participants were screened using the Eysenck Personality Questionnaire (EPQ).
EEG (Electroencephalography): EEG signals were recorded using a 62-channel Neuroscan system. Eye-tracking: Eye movement parameters were synchronously recorded using the Tobii Pro Fusion eye tracker.

The emotion induction is based on 80 video clips (12 clips for each of the following emotions: happiness, sadness, fear, anger, disgust, surprise, and 8 neutral clips). Each video clip has a duration of 2 to 5 minutes. Each participant completes 4 sessions, conducted on different dates. Each session consists of 20 trials. Each session induces 5 of the 7 target emotions to avoid frequent switching between emotional states. Each trial is divided into two parts: Viewing the emotion-inducing video (with a 3-second countdown before and after the video). Self-assessment of emotions: At the end of the session, participants review the video and provide a continuous rating (between 0 and 1). A rating of 1 indicates that the target emotion was strongly induced, while 0 indicates no emotion induction. For neutral videos, a score of 0 is given for emotional fluctuation, and a score of 1 is given for maintaining a natural state.
The raw EEG signal has a sampling rate of 1000 Hz and is downsampled to 200 Hz before feature extraction.

Jiang, W.-B., Liu, X.-H., Zheng, W.-L., & Lu, B.-L. (2025). SEED-VII: A multimodal dataset of six basic emotions with continuous labels for emotion recognition. IEEE Transactions on Affective Computing, 16(2), 969–985. https://doi.org/10.1109/TAFFC.2024.3485057

FENet is a standalone regression wrapper specifically designed for time-series signals. Its core concept lies in addressing feature extraction and dimensionality reduction in multi-channel time series through a network architecture inspired by the wavelet transform. It can efficiently separate and extract key frequency band/scale features from time-series data while preserving global context. From the code implementation and network topology, the present FENet architecture consists of the following core modules: Wavelet Feature Split Block (WaveletBlock) As the underlying basic component, this module performs a single-channel feature/pass split block inspired by FENet. Input data is padded and fed into parallel 1D convolutional layers (feat_conv and pass_conv), respectively. The feature extraction branch is compressed via LeakyReLU activation and adaptive average pooling, while the pass branch applies Dropout and continues to forward the signal to subsequent network layers, thereby achieving layer-by-layer information decoupling and extraction in deep networks. Channel-Shared Backbone Network (FENetBackbone) When processing multi-channel physiological data of shape (B, T, C), the framework adopts a special isolation topology: it applies an identical single-channel convolutional stack independently and repeatedly to every individual neural signal channel (apply same single-channel stack to every neural channel). After each channel undergoes multi-stage WaveletBlock filtering, the extracted multi-scale features are concatenated in the final stage and standardized globally through LayerNorm. Multi-Mode Sequence Decoder Head (Dual-Track / Sequence Decoder Head) To accommodate various time-series tasks, FENetRegressorModel is equipped with an extremely flexible decoding route, which can be freely switched among three modes via the sequence_decoder parameter: Global decoding (Global): Flattens the backbone-extracted features and sends them into a multilayer perceptron (MLP) head composed of fully connected layers (Linear), GELU activation, and Dropout, directly outputting global prediction results after dimensionality reduction. Temporal/hybrid decoding (Temporal/Hybrid): Specially equipped with a temporal decoding head (temporal_head), which replaces fully connected layers with cascaded 1D convolutions (Conv1d) to maintain temporal resolution unchanged. In Hybrid mode, global static feature predictions can also be directly added to temporal dynamic predictions to meet the complex requirements of time-distributed outputs.
