UniBCI (Universal Brain-Computer Interface) is a general-purpose invasive neural signal decoding model based on a hybrid attention mechanism, with its backbone network derived from the large-scale neural population activity pretraining architecture of POYO-1 (Azabou et al., 2024). In the field of brain-computer interfaces (BCI), traditional single-task decoders face significant bottlenecks in generalization across brain regions, species, and behavioral paradigms. The core hypothesis of UniBCI is that by uniformly transforming spike sequences of arbitrary shapes into fixed-length token representations and combining intra-patch and inter-patch dual-stage attention mechanisms, the model can simultaneously capture local temporal dynamics and global cross-channel interactions within a unified representation space, thereby sharing a single pretrained backbone across both classification (discrete intent recognition) and regression (continuous movement decoding) tasks. From the perspective of implementation and network topology, the UniBCI architecture comprises the following core modules: Spike Tokenizer The model does not perform manual feature extraction on raw spike sequences. Instead, it transforms neural data of arbitrary shapes into fixed-dimensional sequence tensors through a unified tokenization pipeline: Channel Grouping: C neural channels are evenly distributed across S spatial tokens (default S=8), with each token accommodating N=group_size neurons (default 32), for a total capacity of S × N = 256 channels. Shortfalls are zero-padded, and excess channels are truncated. Temporal Chunking: Through uniform temporal binning (mean-pooling aggregation by default), the original T time steps are compressed or padded to a fixed length of P×W (P=patch_num=10, W=patch_size=10, totaling 100 time steps). Positional Encoding Generation: Temporal position indices (time_pos) and spatial position indices (space_pos) are automatically generated, along with a padding_mask for under-capacity channels, enabling selective masking in subsequent attention modules. Input Projection and Spatiotemporal Positional Encoding The grouped spike tensor (B, P, W, S, C) passes through two linear projections (C → embed_dim → embed_dim, with ReLU activation) to map binary spikes into dense real-valued representations. The model then injects two independent learnable position embedding tables: Time Embedding: nn.Embedding(max_t, embed_dim), providing absolute temporal information for each time step. Space Embedding: nn.Embedding(max_s, embed_dim), providing channel group identity information for each spatial token. Both embeddings are added directly to the input features and regularized via Dropout, enabling the model to perceive the "when" and "where" of spike events. Optional Text-Conditional Encoding The model supports conditional modulation of representations through textual metadata (species, subject, brain region, task type, experimental session). Specifically, a frozen SentenceTransformer (MiniLM) encodes natural language descriptions into 384-dimensional text vectors, which are then mapped to embed_dim through a frozen two-layer projection network (Linear → LayerNorm → ReLU) and injected additively into the token sequence. This design enables the model to perceive contextual differences in data sources during multi-dataset joint pretraining. Intra-Inter Hybrid Attention Encoder The core backbone is composed of multiple stacked IntraInterBlock layers (default 4), with each block sequentially executing two complementary attention operations: Intra-patch Linear Attention: Linear attention is performed along the temporal dimension W within each patch, using the elu(x)+1 feature map kernel, reducing the standard dot-product attention complexity from O(n²) to O(n). This enables the model to efficiently handle fine-grained temporal dependencies within patches. Inter-patch Sliding Window Attention: Causal-masked sliding window attention is performed along the patch dimension P, with a default window size of 10. Standard scaled dot-product softmax attention is used, allowing each patch to attend only to its neighboring preceding patches, balancing global receptive field with computational efficiency. Feature Fusion: Inter-patch features are broadcast-expanded to the temporal dimension, added to intra-patch outputs, and processed through residual connections and an FFN (two-layer fully connected, expansion ratio 4.0, ReLU activation), achieving deep fusion of cross-scale information. Dual-Track Task Readout Heads To accommodate diverse BCI decoding paradigms, the model configures flexible branching readout logic atop the pretrained backbone via UniBCIWrapper: Classification Readout: The backbone's four-dimensional output features (B, P×S, W, embed_dim) are flattened into a one-dimensional vector and sequentially fed through Linear(dim, 4×embed_dim) → ReLU → Linear(4×embed_dim, num_classes), producing class logits. Regression Readout: The same flattened features pass through Linear(dim, 4×embed_dim) → ReLU → Linear(4×embed_dim, num_outputs × P × W) and are reshaped to (B, P×W, num_outputs), realizing time-distributed sequence regression, precisely adapted for frame-by-frame prediction of continuous kinematic coordinates.

UniBCI's classification and regression tasks share the exact same default hyperparameter configuration. The core differences lie in the optimizer and loss function: classification uses Adam + CrossEntropyLoss, while regression uses AdamW + CosineAnnealingLR + SmoothL1Loss. In the regression task, although the default value of weight_decay is 0, the effective value is enforced via max(weight_decay, 1e-4), meaning even passing 0 results in weight decay of 1e-4. val_ratio only takes effect when no external validation set (val_dataset) is provided, and is used to split a validation set from the training data. pretrained_path supports "auto" to automatically download POYO-1 pretrained weights; set to None for random initialization.

Evaluation Metrics Classification: Accuracy + confusion matrix. Regression: MSE, MAE, R2 computed in the original space after inverse normalization. Optimization Strategies Classification: Adam + CrossEntropyLoss. Regression: AdamW + CosineAnnealingLR + SmoothL1Loss (β=1.0), with gradient clipping (max_norm=1.0) to prevent gradient explosion. Hyperparameter Tuning Channel capacity: max_group_num × group_size (default 256) sets the max channels. For datasets exceeding this, select the highest-variance channel subset; for fewer channels, zero-padding applies automatically — reduce max_group_num to cut useless computation. Temporal receptive field: patch_num × patch_size (default 100). Increase time_size for high-sampling-rate data (>1 kHz) to cover longer behavioral windows; scale down for low-sampling-rate data. Cross-patch attention: attn_window (default 10, i.e., all-to-all). Larger values capture longer-range coupling but linearly increase compute. Linear attention: Intra-patch uses linear attention for O(n) complexity; precision loss is negligible at W=10. Monitor approximation error when patch_size > 50. Text encoding: Only meaningful for multi-dataset joint pretraining. No benefit for single-dataset fine-tuning, only adds inference overhead.

UniBCI trades a unified tokenized representation and hybrid attention mechanism for cross-task, cross-dataset generalization: (1) Universal neural signal decoding: UniBCI is the only model in the framework sharing a classification and regression pretraining backbone. Its unified spike tokenization allows seamless transfer across motor decoding, sensory decoding, and cognitive state recognition without task-specific input layer redesign. (2) Large-scale pretraining and fine-tuning: POYO-1 pretrained weights enable rapid few-shot convergence on small datasets. The text-conditional encoding mechanism further supports joint pretraining on heterogeneous multi-source data, providing a foundation for cross-species, cross-region universal neural decoding models.

BCIJelly visualization software supports recordings from humans, macaques and mice, covering the full range of paradigms supported by BCIJelly. For each species, the recorded brain region (highlighted on a 3D brain, left) is shown alongside the experimental subject and the corresponding behavioral paradigm (right), namely an auditory (speech) task in the right superior temporal gyrus for the human, an eight-direction reaching task in the primary motor cortex for the macaque, and a visual grating task in the primary visual cortex for the mouse. To make BCIJelly accessible beyond command-line use, we developed companion visualization software that combines neural signal inspection, spike detection, electrode localization, and decoder configuration and output visualization in a single graphical interface. The software supports recordings from humans, macaques and mice, and covers the full range of paradigms supported by BCIJelly. For each species, the recording brain region is highlighted on a species-specific 3D brain and shown together with the corresponding behavioral paradigm, with an auditory (speech) task in the right superior temporal gyrus for the human, an eight-direction reaching task in the primary motor cortex for the macaque, and a visual grating task in the primary visual cortex for the mouse. This presentation maps recording sites to brain regions consistently across human, macaque and mouse experiments.

Main interface of the software. Users select recording channels from a list (left), view the recording site on a species-specific 3D brain (center), watch the behavioral video stream from the task (top right), and inspect multichannel local field potential (LFP) traces (bottom) alongside recording statistics including the sampling rate and total duration (right). Its main interface combines several synchronized panels. Users select recording channels from a list, view the recording site on a species-specific 3D brain, watch the behavioral video stream from the task, and inspect multichannel local field potential (LFP) traces alongside recording statistics such as the sampling rate and total duration.

Interactive spike detection. Bandpass cutoffs and threshold levels are set in the Preprocess and Parameters panels (left), and detected spikes are overlaid on the LFP traces (right) for immediate visual feedback. The full BCIJelly spike detection pipeline is also accessible through interactive controls, with adjustable bandpass cutoffs and threshold levels, and detected spikes are overlaid on the LFP traces for immediate visual feedback.

Decoder configuration panel. Compatible decoders, including the MLP, GRU, FENet, LSTM, Transformer, LFADS, Stabilization and Cycle-GAN, can be loaded through the algorithm list and assigned to a chosen task. The software also serves as a front end for the BCIJelly decoder library. Compatible decoders can be loaded through the configuration panel and run on a chosen task.

Classification task. The actual reach direction (Behavior, left) is shown alongside the predicted reach direction selected from eight discrete targets (Decoding, right). To make BCIJelly accessible beyond command-line use, we developed companion visualization software that combines neural signal inspection, spike detection, electrode localization, and decoder configuration and output visualization in a single graphical interface. The software supports recordings from humans, macaques and mice, and covers the full range of paradigms supported by BCIJelly. For each species, the recording brain region is highlighted on a species-specific 3D brain and shown together with the corresponding behavioral paradigm, with an auditory (speech) task in the right superior temporal gyrus for the human, an eight-direction reaching task in the primary motor cortex for the macaque, and a visual grating task in the primary visual cortex for the mouse. This presentation maps recording sites to brain regions consistently across human, macaque and mouse experiments. Its main interface combines several synchronized panels. Users select recording channels from a list, view the recording site on a species-specific 3D brain, watch the behavioral video stream from the task, and inspect multichannel local field potential (LFP) traces alongside recording statistics such as the sampling rate and total duration. The full BCIJelly spike detection pipeline is also accessible through interactive controls, with adjustable bandpass cutoffs and threshold levels, and detected spikes are overlaid on the LFP traces for immediate visual feedback. The software also serves as a front end for the BCIJelly decoder library. Compatible decoders can be loaded through the configuration panel and run on a chosen task. Decoding outputs are displayed side by side with the ground-truth behavior, allowing the decoder predictions to be compared directly with the actual behavior. This comparison is shown both for tasks that involve selecting one of several reach directions (classification) and for tasks that reconstruct a continuous trajectory (regression). The full workflow, from raw signals to decoded behavior, can thus be explored interactively without programming.
The BCIJelly visualization software (version 20251230.1.0.0-alpha.1) is available via Google Drive at https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1QP-nBIPxlg4mrJ7myQ6EIRU63Imnh2Ti?usp=sharing.
This dataset is sourced from Zenodo Record 3854034, which contains invasive neural recordings from the primary motor cortex (M1) of non-human primates performing a center-out cursor control task.
Species: Macaca mulatta Number of subjects: 2

Core Configuration: Most experiments recorded activity from the primary motor cortex (M1) with a total of 96 channels. Extended Configuration: Some experiments implemented dual-area synchronous recordings from both M1 and the primary somatosensory cortex (S1), with a total of 192 channels. Recorded Data: Includes offline sorted spike times. Each physical channel contains up to 5 units (including 1 unsorted hash unit and up to 4 independent sorted units). Behavioral Signals and Kinematic Data: The experiment synchronously captures the subject's movement features through 250 Hz high-frequency sampling, primarily consisting of raw position coordinates from which velocity can be derived. Specifically including: Cursor Position/Velocity: The raw data provides the real-time cursor position in a 2D Cartesian coordinate system (x, y). In regression decoding tasks, velocity and acceleration are often derived by differentiating position with respect to timestamps (t) as kinematic decoding targets. Target Position: Marks the visual target endpoint of the task, used to decode movement intentions.

Behavioral Task: Monkeys voluntarily control their movement velocity to reach for targets arranged in a grid (e.g., an 8×8 grid), with no gaps between grid positions or pre-movement delay. Experimental Task: Center-out cursor reaching task (center-to-target).
